issues.title: Login form inputs lack accessible labels and ARIA attributes
issues.description: The Login page form inputs likely use placeholder text instead of proper <label> elements with htmlFor attributes. Missing aria-live region for error announcements, missing aria-invalid on fields with errors, and no aria-describedby linking error messages to inputs. Screen readers cannot navigate the form.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Login.tsx, in the Login page component, make these specific changes: BEFORE: Form inputs use placeholder text without associated <label> elements, error messages are not announced to screen readers, and inputs lack aria-invalid/aria-describedby attributes. AFTER: Every input has a visible <label> with htmlFor matching the input id. Add aria-invalid={!!error} to email and password inputs. Add aria-describedby pointing to error message element id. Wrap the error message display in a div with role='alert' and aria-live='assertive' so screen readers announce login failures. Add aria-label='Sign in to SiteSync' to the form element. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: ProtectedRoute shows truncated skeleton loader with missing content area
issues.description: The ProtectedRoute loading state renders a sidebar skeleton but the content area JSX appears truncated (code ends mid-template-literal with backslash-n). This means during auth loading, users see an incomplete skeleton or a blank right panel instead of a proper full-page skeleton layout matching the app shell.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx, in the ProtectedRoute component's loading branch (the `if (authLoading)` block), make these specific changes: BEFORE: The content area skeleton after the sidebar placeholder is incomplete — the JSX is truncated and does not render a proper main content skeleton. AFTER: Complete the content area skeleton to include: a top bar skeleton (height 56px, full width), then a content area with padding that shows 4-5 skeleton cards in a grid (matching the dashboard layout geometry per design standards). Ensure the entire loading return block is syntactically complete JSX with no template literal artifacts. The skeleton should use the existing Skeleton component from '../Primitives' with pulse animation. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: PermissionGate loading state returns null causing flash of forbidden content
issues.description: When usePermissions is loading, PermissionGate returns null. This means during the brief loading window, gated content is hidden but then may flash-appear before permissions resolve. More critically, if permissions load slower than children mount, there is a window where permission-gated UI flickers. Should show nothing or a skeleton, never flash content then hide it.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/auth/PermissionGate.tsx, in the PermissionGate component, make these specific changes: BEFORE: When `loading` is true, the component returns `null`, which can cause layout shift when permissions resolve and content appears or disappears. AFTER: When `loading` is true, if `showDenied` is false return `null` (safe — content stays hidden). If `showDenied` is true, return a small inline skeleton placeholder (a div with width 100%, height 32px, backgroundColor colors.surfaceInset, borderRadius borderRadius.md, and opacity 0.5 animation) to prevent layout shift. Also add a comment: '// Never render children while permissions are loading — prevents unauthorized content flash'. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: RequestAccessPage has no actionable CTA — just a dead end
issues.description: The RequestAccessPage component (shown when module access denied) displays a shield icon and text but appears to lack a 'Go Back' or 'Return to Dashboard' button. Users hit a dead end with no way to navigate away except using the browser back button. The PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA requires a 'Request Access' action.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/auth/PermissionGate.tsx, in the RequestAccessPage component, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The component shows an access denied message but has no navigation buttons — users are stuck on a dead-end page. AFTER: Add two buttons below the message text. First button: 'Return to Dashboard' — a primary styled button (backgroundColor colors.primary, color colors.white, padding spacing['2'] spacing['4'], borderRadius borderRadius.md, border 'none', cursor 'pointer', fontSize typography.fontSize.sm, fontWeight typography.fontWeight.medium) that calls `useNavigate()` from react-router-dom to navigate to '/dashboard'. Second button: 'Go Back' — a secondary styled button (backgroundColor 'transparent', color colors.textSecondary, border `1px solid ${colors.borderDefault}`, same sizing) that calls `window.history.back()`. Import useNavigate from react-router-dom at the top of the file. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: ProtectedRoute auth loading skeleton uses fixed sidebar width not responsive
issues.description: The loading skeleton in ProtectedRoute renders a fixed-width sidebar (layout.sidebarWidth = 240px) even on mobile. On screens under 768px, this sidebar skeleton takes up most of the viewport, leaving almost no space for content skeleton. Mobile users see a broken loading layout. Should hide sidebar skeleton on mobile and show a simple full-width skeleton instead.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx, in the ProtectedRoute component's loading skeleton (the `if (authLoading)` return block), make these specific changes: BEFORE: The sidebar skeleton placeholder always renders at fixed 240px width regardless of viewport, breaking the layout on mobile screens under 768px. AFTER: Wrap the sidebar skeleton div in a media query check. Add a CSS class or inline style that sets `display: 'none'` on the sidebar skeleton div when viewport is under 768px. Use a simple approach: add `@media (max-width: 768px)` via a style tag, or more practically, add a className and define the hiding in the component. The simplest fix: add to the sidebar div style `display: window.innerWidth < 768 ? 'none' : 'flex'` (note: this is a static check at render time which is acceptable for a loading skeleton). On mobile, the main content skeleton should take full width. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore enforces granular RLS with per-tool permission matrices and provides a polished login flow with SSO (Okta, Azure AD) plus magic link for field crews. Their ProtectedRoute equivalents never show broken skeletons and always provide clear 'Request Access' flows with admin notification. SiteSync's auth module has the right architecture (PermissionGate, ProtectedRoute, role hierarchy) but the execution has gaps: truncated loading states, missing ARIA attributes, dead-end access denied pages, and no mobile consideration in the auth shell. Fieldwire's mobile-first auth loads instantly with a full-screen branded splash, never showing layout skeletons — a pattern SiteSync should adopt for field crews.
summary: The Authentication & RBAC module has solid architectural bones — PermissionGate, ProtectedRoute with dev bypass, role hierarchy, tenant guards, and organization scoping are all present. However, execution has five critical gaps: (1) the ProtectedRoute loading skeleton is truncated/incomplete, creating a broken loading experience; (2) form accessibility is missing across auth pages (no labels, no ARIA, no screen reader announcements); (3) the PermissionGate returns null during loading which can cause content flicker; (4) the RequestAccessPage is a dead end with no navigation CTA; and (5) the auth loading skeleton is not responsive for mobile. These are all surgical fixes — no new features needed, just completing what exists. The security fundamentals (RLS checks, tenant guards, permission matrices) are architecturally sound but the UI layer that presents them to users needs polish to meet the acceptance criteria for protected routes, error states, and mobile responsiveness.
